old_claim.note: Internet Protocols (TCP/IP, ARPANET) provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: A certification authority digitally signs each X.509 certificate to certify the binding between its subject and public key, so digital signatures are required by the certificate model.

source_shape.source_locator: Sections 4.1.1.2-4.1.1.3 specify the certificate signature algorithm and signature value and explain that the CA signature certifies the subject-to-public-key binding.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: RFC 5280 defines a CA-generated digital signature as part of every X.509 certificate's structure and certification semantics.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: A networking protocol can transport or retrieve certificates, but the certificate remains an X.509 trust object only because its issuing authority signs the identity-to-key binding.

ontology_before: The graph linked PKI directly to TCP/IP while omitting the certificate-signature mechanism that constitutes the X.509 trust model.
ontology_after: Digital signatures are a required direct prerequisite, while TCP/IP remains a later transport context rather than a constituent of PKI.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed and locked by graph invariant 2026-07-16-public-key-infrastructure-x509-boundary: digital_signatures is required and internet_protocols_tcp_ip_arpanet is absent.
would_reject_if: The node is rescoped to a network service that does not issue or validate authority-signed certificates.
short_reason: RFC 5280 makes the CA signature constitutive of an X.509 certificate, whereas TCP/IP is only a possible transport environment.
